titleStmt.title: Letter from Marie Martin to her mother Mary Lewis Martin, 21 June 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Marie Martin

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Letter from Marie Martin (1892-1975) to her mother Mary Lewis Martin (née Moore). In this letter Marie writes about the difficulty and delays of sending and receiving mail. She talks about her daily activities and how she is enjoying her post. She also asks after her two brothers Tommy (b. 1891) and Charlie (1895-1915) who were serving as members of the British army. Charlie had been missing in action since December 1915 on the Salonika front.Marie was later the founder of the Medical Missionaries of Mary. At the outbreak of war in 1914 Marie joined the Voluntary Aid Detachment, a division of the Red Cross. She returned home briefly in 1916 following the death of her brother in battle until be called to serve again two months later in a field hospital in Heraldo France.

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
21st Juneâ 25th General Hospital B. E. F. My very dearest Mother, I hope you received my
letter with my birthday wishes
to you toâday. I must start
off by telling you do not
worry if you do not get
many letters. I shall try
& write you a card every
day, we have to be most
particular what we say. all
our letters are censored, which
takes some time, so that they
may be delayed very much.
I have had a very busy day
so am writing this in bed
& we had a concert here for
the patients in the evening
I did not sing. I do wish
I had taken lessons so that
I could have helped in this
way also. Now that I am
getting to know my sister
I think I shall like my
work very much, of course
we have a great deal mor
house work to do than in
Malta. We gave the few
men that were left toâday
a Tea, but unfortunately it
was very scrappy as it
is impossible to buy any
thing out here. & I have
not had time to get
to Boulogne yet, as one
can only get there when
we have half a day
which I believe are going
to cease owing to the rush
we are expecting any
time nowâThere is so
much I would like to
say but think it safer
not as they are very
particular out hereâ I shall
try and keep a dairy
Imagine I have not seen
a paper since I left
england so do not
know what is really
happening, there seems
to be a fair amount
of fighting as we heard
the guns queit distinctly
all day toâdayâ We were
really busy toâdayâ
My toe is rather trouble
some. I have not been
able to wear any other
shoes than my patent
leather pair as for the
new ones they are
impossible as yet but
am waiting till toe is
alright. I may have to
get a pair of Walking shoes
just like my P. leather ones
only in Cold for the work
it is a very hard place
on shoesâ However I shall
let you knowâ No letters from home yet
we get a Mail every
day, but the last few
days it has been very
late. Will you send
me Bernards & the other
O'Kellys address I may
be fairly near B. baseâ
I am feeling A.1 & live
a very out of door life
really. Paul & I have
a charming room toâgether
in a Villa, we were very
lucky as a great number
have to sleep on Camp
bedsâ Matron seems very
nice so do all the sisters
Did you get the Â£5â00
note I am very worried
about that?
22ndsuperscript text I hope you have had
good news of Tommy lately
I hope they are doing
nothing out there. I suppose
you have heard nothing more
of poor Charlieâ? How are all the family at
home, 28 & poor Aunt May
I very often think of her
& poor Uncle Bernard R.I.P.
we have a sweet little
church here & there is
daily mass here at
6â30 & several on Sunday
so we were able to get
mass toâday â when I
Remember all your intending
also Aunt Maysâ I hope by this you
have heard something
of me but am afraid
mails are badâ
Now I must close as
it is time for lights
outâ With my fondest
love, every loving thought
good wishâ Your fond daughter Marie P.S. I suppose you have
heard nothing of G.G. ?
